title: Bouncing Back
subtitle: Recovery, DR & learning the lesson

hook.metaphor: Surviving the fire is only half the story — then you have to rebuild, move back in, and crucially, figure out *why* it started so it doesn't happen again. Many organisations exhale the moment the immediate crisis passes and skip the rebuild and the lesson — which is exactly why they keep having the same fire. Bouncing back well is what turns a disaster into something you emerge from stronger.
hook.scenario: After a damaging outage, a company restores its systems, everyone celebrates, and they move straight back to business as usual. No review, no root-cause analysis. Six months later, the identical outage happens again. They 'recovered' both times — so why did they learn nothing?

learn: Continuity has a tail end people love to skip: **recovery** — the structured return from 'limping along on Plan B' to normal operations. It's not automatic; restoring systems, reconciling what happened during the disruption, and verifying everything works correctly all take deliberate effort.

On the technical side sits **Disaster Recovery (DR)** — specifically restoring IT systems and data (from the backups and failover you set up earlier). DR is a *subset* of business continuity: BCM keeps the whole business running; DR is the IT-recovery piece within it. Don't confuse the part for the whole.

Recovery should be **prioritised by the BIA**: bring the most critical processes back first (within their RTOs), not whatever's easiest. And it must be **verified** — 'the system is back' isn't the same as 'the system is back *and working correctly*', especially after data loss or a messy failover.

The most-skipped, highest-value step is the **post-incident review / lessons learned** — a blameless analysis of what happened, what worked, what didn't, and what to change. Our company's repeat outage is the signature symptom of skipping this: you can 'recover' endlessly and still never improve (the no-blame, learning-culture thread from Operational Risk).

Done well, recovery closes the loop: the lessons feed back into better plans, fixed root causes, and updated continuity strategies — so each crisis leaves you *more* resilient, not just back where you started waiting for the next identical one.
bridge: So at work, you can make sure a crisis actually makes you stronger by insisting on the two skipped steps: *did we recover the critical things first and verify they truly work — and did we run a blameless review to fix the root cause?* An organisation that keeps suffering the same incident hasn't got a recovery problem; it's got a *learning* problem — it exhaled before it learned.

interactions.title: Recovered twice, learned nothing
interactions.prompt: A company restored systems after an outage, skipped any review, and hit the identical outage six months later. Why?

options.label: Bad luck — lightning struck twice.
options.feedback: It wasn't luck — it was the same root cause, never found or fixed. Skipping the post-incident review guarantees the same fire keeps reigniting.

options.label: They skipped the lessons-learned review, so the root cause was never fixed.
options.feedback: Exactly. Recovery without a blameless post-incident review means you return to normal with the same flaw intact. You can 'recover' forever and never improve.

options.label: They recovered fine both times, so there's no real problem.
options.feedback: Recovering repeatedly from a preventable, recurring incident IS the problem. Without learning, you're just paying for the same disaster again and again.

interactions.title: Recovery vocabulary
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Structured return from Plan B to normal operations
The IT-systems/data recovery piece within BCM
Confirming systems are back AND working correctly
A blameless analysis of what happened and what to fix
Feeding improvements back into plans and root-cause fixes

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Bouncing back means recovering the critical things first and verifying they truly work — and then running a blameless review to fix the root cause — because a company that recovers but never learns just keeps having the same fire.”

checkpoint.q: Disaster Recovery (DR) relates to Business Continuity (BCM) as…
options: DR (IT/data recovery) is a subset of the broader BCM
checkpoint.explain: BCM keeps the whole business running; DR is specifically the IT-recovery piece within it.

checkpoint.q: Repeatedly suffering the same incident usually means the organisation has…
options: A learning problem — it skips the post-incident review and root-cause fix
checkpoint.explain: Recovering without learning leaves the root cause intact, so the same fire keeps reigniting.

options: Assuming the system is fine once it powers on
Confirming systems are back AND actually working correctly
Telling customers it's fixed without checking
checkpoint.explain: 'Back' isn't the same as 'back and correct' — especially after data loss or a messy failover.
